'''Yamada Uenoyama Kofun''' (山田上ノ山古墳) is an ancient {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Taishi, Osaka|ja|太子町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishi, Osaka|1=Taishichō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856365}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|en|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|WD=Q1347323|lt=Minamikawachi District|1=Minamikawachi District, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular tumulus|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and part of the {{ill|Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|ja|磯長谷古墳群|1=Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|qq=}}.

The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} has Historic Site1 it as the "'''Osaka Shinga Mausoleum'''" (大阪磯長陵), the mausoleum of the 36th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}}.

== Overview ==
The mound is located in southeastern Osaka Prefecture, at the foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}}, where it was constructed on a hill extending westward from the mountain. In front of the hill lies the {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹内街道|1=Takenouchi Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it was referred to as the "Kitayama Mausoleum" and was also known as the "Uguisu Mausoleum" (鶯陵).<ref name="Taishi">{{ill|Taishi Town Homepage|ja|太子町ホームページ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishi Town Homepage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref> The {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} links this site to the "Uguisu Mausoleum" mentioned in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}}.<ref group="Note">Some sources attribute the "Uguisu Mausoleum" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}} to {{ill|Uguisuzuka Kofun|en|Uguisuzuka Kofun|WD=Q11675843|lt=Uguisuzuka Kofun|1=Uguisuzuka Kofun}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}} (see "Uguisuzuka Kofun," {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture, Heibonsha, 1981).</ref> Currently, the tomb is under the management of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, and no detailed investigations have been conducted.

The tumulus is circular, with a diameter of 32 meters, and faces southwest. While details of the burial facilities are unclear, the site is known for the {{ill|Sea-beast grape mirror|lt=Sea-beast grape mirror|lt=Sea Beast Grape Mirror|ja|海獣葡萄鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sea Beast Grape Mirror|qq=}} supposedly excavated from a surrounding satellite mound, though its exact location remains unknown. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates it as the tomb of the 36th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}} (who passed away in {{ill|654|en|654|WD=Q31109|lt=654|1=654}}). The modest size of the tumulus may reflect Emperor Kōtoku's adherence to the {{ill|Thin burial Decree|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thin burial Decree|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}}.<ref name="Kotoku">{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Hakuchi 5 (654), October 10 (Jinshi).</ref>

The Shinga Valley is home to other imperial tombs attributed to Emperors {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}, {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}, {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, and Prince {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, collectively referred to as the "Umebachi Mausoleums."

== Interred Individual ==
The actual individual interred at Yamada Uenoyama Kofun is unknown. However, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates it as the mausoleum of the 36th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}}.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/successive_list.html Mausoleums] - {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}.</ref><ref>{{NDLDC|1237123/12|title=Ryo Bo Yoran}} (Imperial Household Ministry, 1934).</ref> According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Kōtoku passed away in the 10th month of Hakuchi 5 (654) and was buried at "Osaka Shinga Mausoleum" in the 12th month of the same year.<ref name="Kotoku"/> The {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, compiled in the 10th century, describes this mausoleum as a "remote tomb" in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, with a precinct of 5 chō (approximately 545 meters) in both east-west and north-south directions, and three caretakers annually assigned to its maintenance.<ref name="Engishiki">{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, vol. 21, Ministry of Ceremonial.</ref> During the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era|1=Genroku}} (1699), the tomb was officially identified as Emperor Kōtoku's mausoleum, and renovations were carried out in {{ill|1864|en|1864|WD=Q7704|lt=1864|1=1864}}.

The mound is located in southeastern Osaka Prefecture, at the foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}}, where it was constructed on a hill extending westward from the mountain. In front of the hill lies the {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹内街道|1=Takenouchi Kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it was referred to as the "Kitayama Mausoleum" and was also known as the "Uguisu Mausoleum" (鶯陵).<ref name="Taishi">{{ill|Taishi Town Homepage|ja|太子町ホームページ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishi Town Homepage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref> The {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} links this site to the "Uguisu Mausoleum" mentioned in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}}.<ref group="Note">Some sources attribute the "Uguisu Mausoleum" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}} to {{ill|Uguisuzuka Kofun|en|Uguisuzuka Kofun|WD=Q11675843|lt=Uguisuzuka Kofun|1=Uguisuzuka Kofun}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}} (see "Uguisuzuka Kofun," {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture, Heibonsha, 1981).</ref> Currently, the tomb is under the management of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, and no detailed investigations have been conducted.

The tumulus is circular, with a diameter of 32 meters, and faces southwest. While details of the burial facilities are unclear, the site is known for the {{ill|Sea-beast grape mirror|lt=Sea-beast grape mirror|lt=Sea Beast Grape Mirror|ja|海獣葡萄鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sea Beast Grape Mirror|qq=}} supposedly excavated from a surrounding satellite mound, though its exact location remains unknown. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates it as the tomb of the 36th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}} (who passed away in {{ill|654|en|654|WD=Q31109|lt=654|1=654}}). The modest size of the tumulus may reflect Emperor Kōtoku's adherence to the {{ill|Thin burial Decree|ja|薄葬令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thin burial Decree|1=Thin Burial Edict|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622174}}.<ref name="Kotoku">{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Hakuchi 5 (654), October 10 (Jinshi).</ref>

The Shinga Valley is home to other imperial tombs attributed to Emperors {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}, {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}, {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, and Prince {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, collectively referred to as the "Umebachi Mausoleums."

== Interred Individual ==
The actual individual interred at Yamada Uenoyama Kofun is unknown. However, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates it as the mausoleum of the 36th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}}.<ref>[https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/successive_list.html Mausoleums] - {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}.</ref><ref>{{NDLDC|1237123/12|title=Ryo Bo Yoran}} (Imperial Household Ministry, 1934).</ref> According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Kōtoku passed away in the 10th month of Hakuchi 5 (654) and was buried at "Osaka Shinga Mausoleum" in the 12th month of the same year.<ref name="Kotoku"/> The {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, compiled in the 10th century, describes this mausoleum as a "remote tomb" in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, with a precinct of 5 chō (approximately 545 meters) in both east-west and north-south directions, and three caretakers annually assigned to its maintenance.<ref name="Engishiki">{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, vol. 21, Ministry of Ceremonial.</ref> During the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era|1=Genroku}} (1699), the tomb was officially identified as Emperor Kōtoku's mausoleum, and renovations were carried out in {{ill|1864|en|1864|WD=Q7704|lt=1864|1=1864}}.
